title: Topic 381: Why User Reviews Differ from Critics: The Taste Gap
content:
### ABSTRACT

In the grand coliseum of public opinion, a perennial drama unfolds: the seasoned, often bespectacled, professional critic duels with the roaring, anonymous crowd of user reviewers. This paper wades into that fray, not to declare a victor, but to ask a more fundamental question: why do they so often disagree? We embark on a humorous yet academically rigorous exploration of the “taste gap,” the chasm separating the meticulous analysis of the expert from the raw, emotional verdict of the consumer. By examining psychological phenomena such as cognitive biases, the potent influence of social proof, and the surprising mechanics of “topic consistency,” this paper dissects the very nature of a five-star rating. We will demonstrate that the difference between a 9/10 from a critic and a 1/10 from a user is more than a matter of opinion; it is a fascinating glimpse into the beautifully flawed, entirely human machinery of how we form judgments in the digital age, proving that sometimes, the most entertaining spectacle isn’t the movie or game itself, but the reviews it leaves in its wake.

### INTRODUCTION

Picture the scene: you, a discerning consumer of culture, have just finished a film the critics hailed as a “masterpiece,” a “triumph of the cinematic form.” You found it… fine. A bit slow, perhaps. The ending felt unearned. You open your favorite review aggregator, ready to have your lukewarm take validated by the masses, only to find a user score so low it suggests the film personally insulted every viewer’s grandmother. Conversely, you might have just spent a joyous weekend with a video game the critics panned as “derivative” and “uninspired,” yet you and thousands of other players are having the time of your lives. This familiar disconnect, this chasm between the professional and the public, is the heart of our inquiry. It is the “taste gap,” and it is more than just a quirky feature of the internet; it is a rich field of study for understanding human behavior.

For decades, the critic was the gatekeeper of culture, a learned authority whose pronouncements could make or break a creative work. But the rise of the internet and platforms like Rotten Tomatoes, Metacritic, and Yelp democratized opinion, giving everyone a voice and a score to brandish. The result has been a fascinating, often hilarious, divergence. The professional critic, armed with a deep knowledge of history, theory, and technique, approaches a work with a scalpel, dissecting its components with analytical precision. The user, on the other hand, often arrives with a heart full of expectation and a wallet recently lightened, their judgment a visceral, emotional response. Is the critic’s measured assessment of a game’s ludonarrative dissonance more valid than a user’s one-star review because the ending made them sad? 

This paper will argue that the answer is not about validity, but about perspective. We will journey through the existing academic literature, with its delightful concepts of “topic consistency” and the surprising power of untrustworthy reviews. We will then dissect the psychological drivers at play, from the critic’s burden of knowledge to the user’s susceptibility to social proof and cognitive dissonance. Through case studies of cultural battlegrounds like *The Last of Us Part II* and *Star Wars: The Last Jedi*, we will see these forces in action. Ultimately, by exploring why user and critic reviews differ, we hold up a mirror to our own wonderfully irrational methods of judgment, consumption, and digital interaction.

### LITERATURE REVIEW

Before we don our lab coats and begin our own amateur psychoanalysis, it is imperative to stand on the shoulders of giants—or at least, to peek over their cubicle walls. The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not been entirely silent on the matter of reviews. One might be surprised to learn that scholars have indeed paused their very important studies of fruit fly mating habits to ponder why we argue so much about movies.

A fascinating study by Jacobs et al. (2015) throws a wrench in our most basic assumption: that a credible review is a powerful review. In a controlled experiment that sounds far more civilized than a Twitter argument, they discovered that the credibility of a review was largely irrelevant to its impact. In fact, it was the *least trustworthy*, but most polarized and negative, review that exerted the biggest influence, primarily by diminishing the viewer's sense of enjoyment and involvement [1]. This suggests that our brains are not dispassionate courts of law weighing evidence, but are more like impressionable teenagers drawn to the loudest, most dramatic voice in the room, even if that voice is demonstrably unreliable. The sober, well-reasoned analysis is often drowned out by the digital shriek.

Further complicating the picture is the work of Deng et al. (2022), who introduced the wonderfully pragmatic concept of “topic consistency” [2]. Their research found that it wasn't just the score, but the *subject* of the conversation that mattered. When both critics and users were discussing the same elements of a film—be it the acting, the plot, or the questionable CGI—the movie's box office revenue saw a significant boost. This effect was most pronounced for films languishing in the purgatory of mediocrity. For a certified masterpiece or an unmitigated disaster, the alignment of conversation mattered less. But for a 6/10 film, a unified chorus of discussion, even if the opinions differed, created a memorable narrative that piqued consumer interest. It seems that for a product to succeed, it helps if everyone is at least arguing about the same thing.

Underpinning all of this is the vast and treacherous terrain of cognitive bias, which scholars like Bedeley, Hao, and Ghoshal (2025) have begun to map within online platforms [3]. In the face of overwhelming information—a digital deluge of opinions, scores, and hot takes—our brains deploy mental shortcuts, or heuristics, to stay afloat. These are the cognitive equivalent of grabbing the nearest, brightest life-raft. This can manifest as the **bandwagon effect**, where the sheer volume of positive or negative user reviews creates a form of **social proof**, convincing us to adopt the majority opinion to feel a sense of belonging. As the old saying goes, “Fifty million Frenchmen can't be wrong,” especially when they’re all leaving one-star reviews. This body of research confirms that our journey through the landscape of online reviews is rarely a rational one; it is a stumble through a funhouse of psychological mirrors, each one distorting our judgment in subtle, predictable ways.

### ANALYSIS

To truly understand the taste gap, we must perform a psychological autopsy on its two main participants: the Critic and the User. They are not merely two people with different opinions; they are often playing entirely different sports on the same field. Their equipment, training, and victory conditions are fundamentally misaligned, leading to the beautiful chaos we see in review scores.

**The Mindset of the Critic: A Burden of Knowledge**

The professional critic operates under a unique set of pressures and learned behaviors. Firstly, their evaluation is inherently **analytical, not purely emotional**. A critic is trained to deconstruct a work into its constituent parts: the cinematography, the editing, the performance, the script, the game mechanics, the level design. They possess a specific vocabulary to articulate *why* a certain element succeeds or fails. A user might say a movie was “boring,” whereas a critic might identify “lethargic pacing in the second act that fails to escalate the narrative stakes.” This analytical framework distances them from a purely visceral reaction. They are the sommeliers of culture, identifying notes of oak and hubris while the user is simply asking, “Does it taste good?”

Secondly, the critic is saddled with the **burden of knowledge**. They have seen and played *everything*. The average consumer might see five films a year; a professional critic sees hundreds. This immense backlog creates a different baseline for novelty and quality. A plot twist that shocks a casual viewer might be a tired trope to a critic who has seen it a dozen times before. They become jaded to convention and, in turn, place a higher premium on innovation and subversion. This is why critics often praise works that “push the boundaries” or “deconstruct the genre,” even if those same qualities alienate a general audience that came looking for a familiar, comforting experience. The critic is searching for the next evolution of the art form; the user is often just looking for a good time.

**The Mindset of the User: The Heart Wants What It Wants**

The user, on the other hand, is a gloriously emotional creature. Their primary metric for evaluation is often pure, unadulterated **enjoyment**. Did the game make them feel powerful? Did the movie make them cry? Was it *fun*? Their investment is not just time, but often money and emotional expectation. This leads to several predictable psychological phenomena.

Chief among them is **social proof**. As established, when faced with uncertainty, people look to the actions of others to guide their own behavior. The aggregate user score on Metacritic or Rotten Tomatoes becomes a powerful beacon. A flood of negative reviews (a practice known as “review bombing”) can create a self-fulfilling prophecy, signaling to potential players that the consensus is negative and coloring their own perception before they even begin. It becomes a collective act, a way to voice displeasure and feel part of a larger movement, divorced from the intrinsic quality of the product itself.

Furthermore, the user is susceptible to **cognitive dissonance**, the mental discomfort experienced when holding conflicting beliefs. A person who just spent $70 on a new video game and waited months for its release is highly motivated to believe their purchase was a good one. If the game is flawed, they are more likely to either downplay its faults or overstate its virtues to justify their investment. This can lead to fiercely defensive user reviews that seem to be reviewing the *decision to purchase* as much as the game itself. Conversely, if a user feels betrayed by a product (e.g., a sequel that deviates from its predecessor), their review becomes an outlet for that sense of betrayal, an emotional response that a detached critic is less likely to share.

Synthesizing these two mindsets reveals the “taste gap” in its full glory. It is the chasm between the critic’s analytical, novelty-seeking, context-rich evaluation and the user’s emotional, investment-driven, socially-influenced experience. One is judging the craft; the other is judging the feeling. And while both are valid, they are fundamentally different measures of worth.

### REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

Theory is delightful, but the digital battlefield of public opinion is where these psychological forces truly come to life. The taste gap is not an abstract concept; it is written in the stark, glowing numbers of review aggregators for all to see. A few prominent case studies serve as perfect illustrations of this phenomenon in action.

**Case Study 1: The magnum opus vs. the betrayal - *The Last of Us Part II***

Few releases in modern gaming history showcase the critic-user divide as starkly as Naughty Dog’s 2020 sequel, *The Last of Us Part II*. On the critics’ side, the game was a monumental achievement, garnering near-universal acclaim and a Metascore of 93. Reviewers lauded its technical brilliance, mature and challenging narrative, and the risks it took with its beloved characters. They analyzed its themes of cyclical violence and trauma with academic reverence. For the critics, it was a bold step forward for the medium.

The user score, however, tells a different story. It sits at a 5.8, the result of a massive “review bombing” campaign that began almost the moment the game was released. The source of the outrage was not technical glitches or poor gameplay, but a deep sense of emotional betrayal. The game made controversial narrative choices, particularly concerning the fate of a major character from the first game, that left many players feeling angry and disrespected. Their reviews were not an analysis of the game’s mechanics, but an outpouring of grief and frustration. They had formed a deep emotional bond with the characters, and the sequel’s subversion of their expectations felt like a personal attack. It is the quintessential example of the analytical critic praising a work for its challenging nature, while the emotionally invested user rejects it for that very same reason.

**Case Study 2: Subverting the space opera - *Star Wars: The Last Jedi***

The taste gap is not confined to gaming. Rian Johnson’s 2017 film, *Star Wars: The Last Jedi*, created a similar firestorm in the film community. Critics largely praised the movie, awarding it a 91% “Certified Fresh” rating on Rotten Tomatoes. They celebrated its willingness to deconstruct the myths of Star Wars, to subvert fan expectations, and to take the franchise in a new, unpredictable direction. It was seen as a thoughtful, visually inventive blockbuster that wasn't afraid to challenge its audience.

Audiences, however, were far less enthused, landing the film with a 42% audience score. Many long-time fans felt the film was actively disrespectful to the established lore and characters, particularly the portrayal of Luke Skywalker. Their connection to the franchise was built on decades of established archetypes and emotional investment. The film’s deconstructionist approach felt less like a bold new vision and more like a cynical dismantling of something they held dear. The critics saw a filmmaker taking risks; many fans saw a filmmaker breaking their favorite toys.

**Case Study 3: The inverse case - *Joker***

Interestingly, the taste gap can also be inverted. Todd Phillips’ *Joker* (2019) presents a more complex picture. The film received a more mixed reception from critics (a 68% on Rotten Tomatoes), with many expressing concern over its portrayal of mental illness and violence, while still praising the central performance. The audience score, however, was a robust 88%. Here, the raw, unsettling emotional journey of the protagonist resonated powerfully with audiences, who saw it as a gritty, realistic, and empathetic character study. While some critics were caught up in analyzing the film’s potential social impact and its place within the comic book genre, audiences responded to its visceral, emotional core. It demonstrates that when a work taps directly into a powerful, unfiltered emotion, it can sometimes bypass critical skepticism and forge a direct, powerful connection with the public.

### DISCUSSION

The persistent and often cavernous gap between critic and user opinion is more than a source of entertainment for those of us who enjoy watching strangers argue on the internet. It holds a mirror up to the way we consume culture, the way we form opinions, and the very nature of a shared experience in a fragmented digital world. The implications of this taste gap ripple outwards, affecting not just the consumer, but the creators caught in the crossfire.

For the humble consumer, the key takeaway is the need for **critical literacy**. This does not mean everyone needs to become a professional critic, but rather that we must learn to consume reviews with an awareness of the biases at play, both in the reviewer and in ourselves. Understanding that a critic may be rewarding novelty while a user is punishing a deviation from expectation allows us to triangulate our own potential experience. The wise consumer does not blindly follow the highest score, but seeks out a variety of voices—critics and users—whose described experiences and evaluative frameworks seem to align with their own. It is about finding *your* critic, not *the* critic, and recognizing that a user score is often a measure of collective emotion as much as it is a measure of quality.

For the creators—the game developers, the filmmakers, the authors—the taste gap presents a daunting challenge. Who is your audience? Do you create for the analytical critic, hoping for awards and prestige, or for the passionate fanbase, hoping for commercial success and loyalty? The case studies of *The Last of Us Part II* and *The Last Jedi* show the perils of subverting fan expectations, even when it results in critical acclaim. It suggests that for established franchises with emotionally invested audiences, radical change is a high-risk, high-reward strategy. This can lead to a chilling effect, where creators become risk-averse, producing safe, predictable content designed to appease the broadest possible user base, thereby stifling the very innovation that critics champion.

Ultimately, the taste gap is a microcosm of our broader digital society. It reflects the formation of echo chambers, where like-minded individuals reinforce each other’s opinions, and the polarization that results when differing worldviews collide. The ability for a user to drop a 0/10 on a masterpiece of cinema because the ending wasn’t to their liking is a testament to the democratization of opinion, but also to the decline of nuanced discourse. In the end, the endless debate is not just about whether a movie is “good” or “bad.” It is a reflection of our beautifully complex, deeply personal, and often hilariously irrational relationship with art.

### CONCLUSION

In our journey through the treacherous landscape of online reviews, we have found that the chasm between the critic and the user is not a simple matter of one being “right” and the other “wrong.” Rather, it is a complex interplay of psychology, sociology, and the unique pressures of the digital age. The critic, a seasoned cartographer of culture, is mapping the terrain with an analytical eye, rewarding innovation and charting the evolution of the art form. The user, a passionate tourist, is simply trying to have a good time, their experience colored by emotional investment, social pressures, and the very human need to feel their journey was worthwhile.

We have seen that our brains, when faced with an avalanche of information, will cling to the loudest voice, not necessarily the most credible one. We have learned that it helps to at least be arguing about the same thing, as “topic consistency” can turn a mediocre product into a memorable one. And we have witnessed, through the battlegrounds of *The Last of Us Part II* and *The Last Jedi*, how a clash of expectations can create a cultural schism.

So, the next time you find yourself bewildered by a review score, remember the taste gap. Recognize that the person on the other side of the screen may not be a fool or a philistine, but simply a player in a different game with a different rulebook. In the end, the most enduring truth is that art is subjective, and in the grand, chaotic democracy of the internet, every voice gets a vote, even if they’re all shouting at once.
references:
[1] Jacobs, R. S., Heuvelman, A., Allouch, S. B., & Peters, O. (2015). Everyone's a critic: The power of expert and consumer reviews to shape readers’ post-viewing motion picture evaluations. *Poetics*, *52*, 58-73. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.poetic.2015.07.002

[2] Deng, T., Zheng, J., Khern-am-nuai, W., & Kannan, K. N. (2022). Does Topic Consistency Matter? A Study of Critic and User Reviews in the Movie Industry. *Journal of Marketing*, *86*(6), 139-158. https://doi.org/10.1177/00222429221127927

[3] Bedeley, R. T., Hao, H., & Ghoshal, T. (2025). Cognitive Biases in Online Opinion Platforms: A Review and Mapping. *SAGE Open*, *15*(1). https://doi.org/10.1177/21582440251315564
